reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Monday in July. Nothing dramatic about it. The long weekend is over — maybe you had one, maybe you worked through it — and now there's a week of ordinary hours ahead. This is where sobriety actually lives. Not in the big anniversaries or the dramatic before-and-after stories, but in what you do on a Monday morning when nobody is watching and nothing particular is at stake.

When I was drinking, Mondays were a kind of penance. I white-knuckled through the aftermath of whatever the weekend had been, promised myself things, and by Wednesday I was already negotiating. The week wasn't a thing I lived — it was a thing I survived on my way back to Friday. What I didn't understand at 22 is that a life is mostly Mondays. If you can't show up for those, you don't really have a life; you have a countdown between escapes.

Durant's line about habit isn't motivational poster material — it's mechanical. You become the things you repeat. Coffee, made. Bed, made. Text back to the friend, sent. Run, done. Meeting, attended. None of it feels like much on the day. But stack enough ordinary Mondays and one afternoon you look up and realize you've built something. Not a highlight reel. A life you don't need to escape from.
reflection.meditation: What is one small, ordinary thing I can do today that the drinking version of me would have skipped?

affirmations: I am building a life out of ordinary days, and today is one of them.
Today I show up for the small things that used to feel beneath me.
I am someone who follows through, one hour at a time.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a quiet Monday I don't have to claw my way through. Grateful for coffee that tastes like coffee, a clear head, and work I can actually do. Grateful for the friends who text on random weekdays, and for the version of me who kept going long enough to have them.
gratitude.closing: One more ordinary day. That's the whole gift.
